notes.anchor: what not many new men have
notes.note: A novus homo (new man) was one who reached high office without consular ancestors, as Fadius (and Cicero himself) had. The consolation turns on the topos that Fadius's career was a self-made achievement beyond his birth, so his fall costs him less than it would a born noble.

Quam levissima poena: Cicero's bleak claim that, given the corruption of the courts in the late 50s BC, the man penalised most lightly is best off having quit public life. The remark reflects his disillusion with the criminal courts after his own and others' condemnations.

notes.anchor: carried by a single vote, and that a doubtful one
notes.note: Cicero minimises Fadius's conviction by insisting the jury split was razor-thin and that the deciding vote was suspect, the product of some powerful man's influence (potentia). Roman jury verdicts were decided by a simple count of tablets, so a one-vote margin could be cast as no true verdict at all.
